commonIssues: Frost-tender (USDA zones 10-11); plant outdoors only after all frost danger passes and soil reaches about 16C (60F).
Overwatering and poorly drained soil cause stem and root rot and edema; let the top 2.5-5 cm (1-2 in) of soil dry before watering and never let pots sit in water.
Bacterial leaf spot, botrytis (gray mold) blight, and leaf spots; avoid overhead watering and wetting foliage.
Aphids, whiteflies, spider mites, caterpillars, and tobacco budworm.
Leggy growth and leaf drop indoors from insufficient light; bright south/west window or supplemental light needed.
Recommended container minimum is a single-plant pot (about 6-inch / 3.8 L); larger pots hold more water and reduce wilting, so size up if plants wilt frequently.

source.citation: Clemson Cooperative Extension HGIC, Annual Geranium (Pelargonium): at least 4 hours direct sun daily (midday shade in very hot areas); allow soil to dry between waterings; keep water off foliage; fertilize every 4 to 6 weeks during the growing season with 10-20-10 or equivalent; use large, well-drained containers; plant after frost danger and soil warming; problems include bacterial leaf spot, botrytis blight, edema, aphids, caterpillars, mites, whiteflies.

University of Minnesota Extension, Growing geraniums as annual flowers in Minnesota: minimum 8 hours sun (less sun = fewer flowers); water when top 1-2 inches of soil is dry; geraniums tolerate dry soil better than overly wet; fertilize roughly every 3 weeks at half strength with all-purpose water-soluble fertilizer; minimum 6-inch pot with drainage; plant outdoors when soil reaches 60F; dormant overwinter storage at 45-50F watering monthly.

Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder, Pelargonium x hortorum: USDA hardiness zones 10-11; full sun to part shade; medium water needs; height/spread 1-3 ft shrubby mounds; frost-tender; suitable for containers, hanging baskets and window boxes; poorly drained soils lead to stem and root rots; susceptible to leaf spots, gray mold, whiteflies, aphids, tobacco budworm.

Royal Horticultural Society (RHS) Pelargonium Growing Guide: water moderately spring-summer and sparingly in winter, letting compost dry between waterings; feed every 10-14 days with balanced fertilizer then switch to high-potassium (tomato) feed once flowers form; minimum 7-10C and frost-free to overwinter; soil-based (John Innes No.2) or multipurpose compost; problems include grey mould, rust, whitefly, vine weevil.

Confidence note: heatStressC (32C) and coldHardyMinC (5C) are conservative estimates. Sources establish frost-tenderness and USDA zone 10-11 hardiness (Missouri Botanical Garden) and recommend midday shade in 'very hot areas' (Clemson) but give no exact Celsius heat-stress or cold-survival thresholds; UMN cites 45-50F (7-10C) dormant storage and 60F (16C) planting soil. The container recommendedMinLiters (3.8 L / ~1 gal) is derived from UMN's 6-inch minimum pot width converted to a realistic single-plant volume.
